author: @VeniceInventors
text:
SculptGL is quite neat. I paused the video when Robert said "go to the web", I opened Sculpt GL and managed to sculpt an almost anatomically correct head in no time.
Ultimately it's all about using the right tool for any given task. Blender is like the fake swiss army knife of multimedia, it does a little of everything but doesn't do anything really well. It's great for artistic creations and not so great for realistic recreations, but it's free.

author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
text:
Blender really is hard, but the more you learn about it, it really becomes a great tool for many things.
All you really need to know about blender for 3D printing is how to use edit mode and modifiers.
I used blender for almost 10 years and there is still things I don't know how to do properly.
The sculpt mode in blender however is pretty straight forward.

author: @alaingrignon
text: As a beginner I had started using Shapr 3D, which I still love as it makes it easy to design right on my iPad with the pencil but unfortunately it was not parametric which made it difficult to edit, or test different options. So I bit the bullet and have been learning Fusion 360 which I would say is not as a easy is Tinker Cad but not as hard as Blender either. I will say though, that it’s more for engineering and less for sculpting so this little tool will come in handy in those instances where a filet or chamfer just can get you to the shape you really want. Thanks again Rob for all these little nuggets of gold we all can use!

author: @technosaurus3805
text:
For those of us who are impatient, you can use a soldering iron or wood burning tool to melt excess to be scraped away with your favorite blade... you can add/fill the same way using filament like solder.
It probably won't look pretty but it beats waiting 24-48 hrs for each reprint.

author: @0101-f4d
text: I know this will be poorly received but nothing is daunting if you break it into chunks. It never takes more than 10 minutes for me to find out how Blender does a specific thing but once I do it is a new string to my Blender bow. The fact that Blender has such broad capabilities means that not only do my new skills help with the specific task at hand but they can later be extended to projects that I may otherwise have ruled out as unachievable. Maybe I don't want a rigged animation now but if for any reason I decide later that I do it will be trivial to implement.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't disagree with you mate - what i find is a lot of people just don't want to do that - what i am pointing out is a lot of folks just want to do a simple task and don't want to learn a lot of stuff they will never us - nothing wrong with that - equally a lot of folks do want want to do that and nothing wrong with that either - its horses for courses mate

replies.author: @0101-f4d
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I don't get this "stuff you'll never use" line. The only reason I would learn a new feature is because I need to use it. If you are just studying random features of Blender that have no relation to the task at hand then I think I've spotted the problem.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Don't worry about it - just try to understand different people have different ideas and your ideas are not always the best for everyone

replies.author: @0101-f4d
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering A little condescending but noted. It is no skin off my nose if you want to use an inefficient workflow. Sorry if you feel like I am pressuring you to do anything, that is not my intent. I was just trying to understand the logic behind something you said that I do not understand. Obviously "stuff you'll never use" is just one of those things you say to make yourself feel better about not doing something that deep down you know you should do. Sometimes one's ideas are not even the best for oneself.

author: @likemanner
text: Click the Sculpting tab at the top of Blender. Press G for the grab tool. Hold control of you want to push/pull perpendicular to the surface. There are many other useful tools. Smooth (or hold shift) to get rid of bumps. It helps to adjust the Strength (blue bar at the top). Adjust the other slider or hold F to adjust the size of your brush. 🙂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: and you think that is it don't you - i am sorry mate - there is a lot more to it in blender when you are a newcomer and you know that - which makes your post a bit disingenuous lol

replies.author: @st33ldi9ital
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering It's really not that bad, nowhere near as bad as you are making it out to be. Blender is actually pretty easy (for basic things like modelling) once you find your way around the UI and get used to the hotkeys. If you dabble with small projects or tasks like you did with tinkercad a few times a week or month then you'll get it in no time. The hard part of blender comes from other aspects of it... nodes, unwrapping, texturing, rigging, etc. For basic modelling... it's really quite intuitive and easy. I started with autocad, have used tinkercad quite extensively, and now find blender easier and producing better results. There are some really good cad like addons for blender as well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't find it that bad mate - I find that other people find it that bad - personally i think there are lots of ways to approach a problem and some suit some folks and some suit others - in my opinion it's good to have alternatives to find one that suits you rather than banging on one drum so to speak

replies.author: @likemanner
replies.text: Yeah, it's kind of overwhelming the first time you open it up, which led to me giving up the first time I installed it and then coming back to it years later. He is right that once you grasp the interface and the basic features (and the ones you can ignore), it becomes more intuitive. But if you're just modeling/sculpting it makes sense to use a specialized software.

replies.author: @cheyannei5983
replies.text:
I agree with Robert, coming from a modo user. Blender's UX is absolutely atrocious and the new 3.0 update hasn't fixed much because a loud minority of contributors want to keep it the way it is. There's also no easy way to keep UX or UI packages up to date without making it a full time job to integrate every new tool or button combination, and again there's been no movement from the Blender Foundation on growing a plugin ecosystem.

It's just much harder to use for no reason. So few of Blender's tools are better than their paid competitors and when they are, don't fit into workflows with other programs or apps. Blender is fine if all of your 3D work never leaves the program.

It's great that Blender is free and accessible, but every other program has to work together or nobody buys the software. At the end of the day, 3D modeling is a job and people only have a finite time on this Earth.

author: @ImpossibumPlays
text: I was going to suggest a slightly more advanced CAD program like Onshape but honestly I think Sculpt GL is likely a more intuitive solution. The trouble with being able to do everything is that you have to wade through all the muck to get to what you need which it can be quite overwhelming. Glad to see you still expanding your horizons while keeping the interesting content coming. Keep up the good work!

author: @10sheds21
text: I've yet to embark on this voyage but I've just bought my granddaughter a 3d pen  and I was wondering if you had imperfections could you titivate it with a pen or would it just drop off. Very interesting video. Thanks

replies.author: @zapaholic
replies.text: You can titivate with it, you can weld parts together and fill in holes.  Assuming you're using the same material that the original part was made of, you can get great results using a 3d pen on printed objects.

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text:
You can even just use a soldering iron or wood burning kit with your filament and some little sculpting blades.
They do get unnecessarily hot though so you'll need to move quickly and precisely, but it can also smooth out layer lines - great for lost PLA casting.
